# General Policy Work

```{r knit-options, include=FALSE, cache=FALSE}
type = 5
load(here::here("data", type, "means.Rdata"))
here::here("code", "formatting.R") |> source()
```

> ABSTRACT: Stark differences in the amount of policy work legislators do is driven by the capacity gained through institutional positions. Like with constituency service, experience matters, independent of institutional positions of power, but institutional power drives differences in policy work to a degree much greater than any kind of constituency service. Unsurprisingly, being a committee chair, ranking member, or oversight committee member is an especially strong predictor of contacting an agency about policy. Like with constituency service, legislators are more likely to work with agencies on policy when they are ideologically aligned, reflecting how policy work is not just traditional “oversight” investigations into wrongdoing, but also includes proactive information seeking during the drafting of legislation and, most commonly, follow-up to make sure legislative directions result in agency rules as envisioned by the legislators. Unlike constituency service, where legislators are more likely to contact co-partisan presidents, the partisanship of the president has a less significant effect on policy work.


## What it looks like

```{r}
#| label: fig-words
#| fig-cap: "Most frequent words by year"

knitr::include_graphics(here::here("figs", "wordcloud", type, "overall_by_year.png"))
```

As shown in the word cloud of frequently used words in @fig-words, members of Congress communicate with federal agencies about general policy work involving a variety of matters related to policy, including hearings, rules, and programs that are part of policy implementation, as well as requests for information about specific policy issues. Many of the specific policy issues seem to follow the "events of the day": "Katrina" appears in 2005; "ARRA" (the American Recovery and Reinvestment Act, or stimulus bill) appears in 2009; and "Ebola" appears in 2014, along with "Zika" in 2016 and "COVID" in 2020. The Affordable Care Act also had a major presence in congressional correspondence between 2010 and 2012. 

Legislators' communications with federal agencies for the purpose of general policy work speak to Congress's role in conducting oversight of the bureaucracy. Senator Chuck Grassley (R-IA), the most prolific author of policy-related correspondence, sent a number of letters to the Centers for Medicare and Medicaid Services (CMS) within the Department of Health and Human Services that were tagged by the agency as "oversight." These letters covered topics such as budget sequestration, the implementation of the Sunshine Act, and various rules, including the Inpatient Prospective Payment System (IPPS) rule, the Medicare Hospital Value-Based Purchasing rule, and CMS's delay in drafting other regulations required by the Affordable Care Act. Grassley also co-authored a letter with two other unidentified legislators to request information about the Patient Safety and Medical Liability Reform Demonstration and Planning grants that CMS was funding. All of the examples discussed here occurred during the Obama administration. Overall, many of Grassley's communications with CMS seem to be focused on conducting oversight of an agency with which he was not ideologically aligned--a departure from the findings in our analysis, as we discuss in greater detail below. 

The most frequent words used in congressional contacts with the Mine Health and Safety Administration, illustrates two important points (1) policy work often means involvement in executive-branch policymaking, and (2) policy work is often intimetly related to constituent service. 


```{r}
#| label: fig-words-msha
#| fig-cap: "General Policy-Related Contacts with the Department of Labor Mine Health and Safety Administration"

knitr::include_graphics(here::here("figs", "wordcloud", type, "by-agency", "DOL_MSHA.png"))
```

```{r}
#| label: fig-percent
#| fig-cap: "Observed levels of interbranch representation per legislator per year"
#| fig-subcap: 
#|   - "Senate"
#|   - "House"
#| layout-ncol: 2
#| out-width: 100%

knitr::include_graphics(here::here("figs", type, "percentile-1.png"))
knitr::include_graphics(here::here("figs", type, "percentile-2.png"))

```

## Explaining Differences in Policy Work

Variation in policy work is mainly driven by ... 


::: {.fake-xref-table-for-caption}

```{=latex}
\begingroup\fontsize{1pt}{1pt}\selectfont\setlength{\tabcolsep}{0pt}\setlength{\arrayrulewidth}{0pt}\setlength{\abovecaptionskip}{0pt}\setlength{\belowcaptionskip}{0pt}
```

|   |
|---|
|   |

: The effect of constituent demand, legislator capacity, and political environment on requests to federal agencies {#tbl-models-full}

```{=latex}
\endgroup\vspace{-2\baselineskip}
```
:::

```{r}
load(here::here("models", type,  "models_full.Rdata"))

modelsummary(models_full,
             notes = list(
               "Robust standard errors in parentheses, clustered by legislator.",
               "This table shows estimates of the effect of constituent demand (population, employment, education, public assistance), legislator capacity (chamber, reporting committee, committee chair, subcommittee chair, and ranking member positions and years in office) and institutional positions that may affect strategic behavior (reporting committee membership, perceived ideological distance from teh agency, co-partisan president) on levels of interbranch representation.")) |>
  # get rid of formatting to get rid of the extra line 
  theme_empty() |> 
  # bold header, hline bottom, aligned center
  tinytable::style_tt(j = 1, align = "l") |>
  tinytable::style_tt(i = 0:1, bold = T, line = "b",  align = "c") |>
  tinytable::style_tt(j = 1, align = "l")  |>  
  # new stuff for this table 
  tinytable::theme_latex(multipage = TRUE) |> 
  group_tt(i = list(
    "Constituent Demand"= 2,
    "Strategic Choices Shaped by Political Environment" = 10,
    #"Political Environment + Capcacy" = 26,
    "Capacity" = 26
  )) |>
  style_tt(i = 44, line = "t", line_color = "black", line_width = 0.1) |>
  style_tt("groupi", background = "grey") |>
  style_tt("~groupi", j = 1, indent = 1)
```

\clearpage


### Demand


```{r}
#| label: fig-pop-effect
#| fig-cap: "Predicted levels of interbranch representation per Senator per year compared to counterfactual of equal state population"
#| out-width: 50%

knitr::include_graphics(here::here("figs", type, "m-population-Senate-effect-1.png"))

#TOD college grads and government workers 

```


### Capacity

Columns 1, 3, and 5 of @tbl-models-full show differences across legislators with different capacity advantages. 


```{r}
#| label: fig-capacity-effect
#| fig-cap: "Predicted levels of interbranch representation per legislator per year compared to counterfactuals"
#| fig-subcap: 
#|   - ""
#|   - ""
#|   - ""
#|   - ""
#| layout-ncol: 2
#| out-width: 100%

knitr::include_graphics(
here::here("figs", type,
           paste0("m-capacity-effect-",
                  c(1:2,5,8), 
                  ".png"
                  ) 
           ))
```




##### Staff prior experience 

```{r}
#| label: tbl-models-district
#| tbl-cap: "The effect of capacity on total requests to federal agencies per district"

load(here::here("models", type,  "models_district.Rdata"))

modelsummary(models_district,
             notes = list("Robust standard errors in parentheses, clustered by district",
                          "This table shows estimates of the effect of electing a new member of the same or different party on levels of interbranch representation.")) 
```


### Strategic Responses to the Environment


#### Interbranch representation as electoral strategy





```{r}
#| label: fig-elect-effect
#| fig-cap: "Predicted levels of interbranch representation compared to counterfactual of no electoral competition"
#| fig-subcap: 
#|   - ""
#|   - ""
#| layout-ncol: 2
#| out-width: 100%

knitr::include_graphics(here::here("figs", type, "m-elect-effect-1.png"))
knitr::include_graphics(here::here("figs", type, "m-elect-effect-2.png"))

```



#### Strategic responses to partisan institutions


 


```{r}
#| label: fig-ideo-effect
#| fig-cap: "Predicted levels of interbranch representation compared to counterfactual of no difference in legislator ideology (a) and perceived agency ideology (b)"
#| fig-subcap: 
#|   - ""
#|   - ""
#| layout-ncol: 2
#| out-width: 100%

knitr::include_graphics(here::here("figs", type, "m-ideo-effect-1.png"))
knitr::include_graphics(here::here("figs", type, "m-ideo-effect-2.png"))
```




```{r}
#| label: fig-partisan-effect
#| fig-cap: "Predicted levels of interbranch representation compared to counterfactuals of no partisan effects"
#| fig-subcap: 
#|   - ""
#|   - ""
#|   - ""
#|   - ""
#| layout-ncol: 2
#| out-width: 100%

knitr::include_graphics(here::here("figs", type, "m-partisan-effect-1.png"))
knitr::include_graphics(here::here("figs", type, "m-partisan-effect-2.png"))
```




#### Staff allocation reflects priories


```{r}
#| label: tbl-models-staff
#| tbl-cap: "The effect of staff budgeting on requests to federal agencies"
#| out-width: 100%

load(here::here("models", type,  "models_staff_spending.Rdata"))

modelsummary(models_staff_spending,
             notes = list("Robust standard errors in parentheses, clustered by legislator.",
                          "This table shows estimates of the effect of legislator staff spending. ")) 
```



```{r}
#| label: fig-effect-staff
#| fig-cap: "Predicted levels of interbranch representation per representative per year compared to counterfactual staffing levels"
#| fig-subcap: 
#|   - ""
#|   - ""
#| layout-ncol: 2
#| out-width: 100%

knitr::include_graphics(
here::here("figs", type,
           paste0("m-staff_spending-effect-",
                  c(1:2), 
                  ".png"
                  ) 
           ))
```
